lines.en_lit: who chews on barley of teruma pays the principal and not fifth. “eats” which excludes one who harms

Rav Sheizvi said Rabbi Yoḥanan said: a non-priest who swallowed plums of teruma and vomited them out, another ate them, the first pays the principal plus a fifth. the second pays only the price of wood, to the first

food and drink do not combine. Who taught Rav Ḥisda said: is taught as a dispute, and is Rabbi Yehoshua. As we learned Rabbi Yehoshua stated a general principle: Any that identical its ritual impurity, and its measure combines
its ritual impurity but not its measure, or if its measure but not its ritual impurity, neither its ritual impurity nor its measure do not combine.

Rav Naḥman said: Even you say the Rabbis, until now we have the Rabbis say there only with respect to ritual impurity. Since the of ritual impurity is one concept, But here, due to settling the mind, and this does not settle the mind.

And similarly, Reish Lakish said: is taught as a dispute. Rabbi Yehoshua, as we learned Rabbi Yehoshua stated a general principle And Rabbi Yoḥanan said Even you say the the Rabbis, until now we have the Rabbis say there only with regard to ritual impurity. But here, due to settling the mind, and this does not

MISHNA: one ate and drank within one lapse of awareness, he is liable only one sin-offering. he ate and performed labor he is liable to bring two sin-offerings, he ate foods that are not fit for eating, or drank liquids that are not fit for drinking, or drank brine or liquid he is exempt,

GEMARA: Reish Lakish said: Why is there no warning stated about affliction; because it was not possible How could write it? Let the Merciful One write: One shall not eat eating the amount of an olive-bulk, Let the Merciful One write: Do not be afflicted. mean: Get up and eat.
Rav Hoshaya strongly objects to this: Let the Merciful One write Guard yourself lest you not be afflicted. If so, there would be many negative mitzvot,

Rav Beivai bar Abaye strongly objects to this: Let the Merciful One write: Guard yourself in the mitzva of affliction. If so, guard yourself about a prohibition, a prohibition; guard yourself about a positive mitzva, a positive mitzva. Rav Ashi strongly objects to this: Let the Merciful One write: Do not stray from afflicting yourself, difficult.

And a tanna cites from here: “And you shall afflict your souls, you shall do no manner of labor” might extension of labor, is punishable the verse states: “And whatsoever soul it be that does any manner of labor on that same day” on that very day is punishable by karet, but the extension of labor is not punishable by karet.

might extension of labor is not punishable by karet, extension of affliction is punishable by karet. the verse states: “For whatsoever soul it be that shall not be afflicted on that same day, he shall be cut off on that very day punishable by karet, but the extension of affliction is not punishable by karet.

might that not be included in the punishment but he would be warned against extension of labor the verse states: “And you shall do no manner of labor on that same day” he is warned that very day, he is not warned against extension of labor,

might He would not be warned with regard to extension of labor but he would be warned with regard to extension of affliction and a logical derivation Just as labor, whose is observed on Shabbatot and Festivals, one is not warned about is it not logical that affliction, which is not observed on Shabbatot and Festivals, he should not be warned about

But the warning during the very day itself we have not learned. From where need not state the punishment labor, since one can learn it from affliction, Just as affliction, which is not observed on Shabbatot and Festivals, is punishable by karet labor, which is observed on Shabbatot and Festivals, not all the more so? why stated It is available to compare and learn a verbal analogy from it: A punishment affliction is stated, and a punishment labor is stated. Just as labor one is punished only if he was warned so too, affliction, one is punished only if he was warned.
This be refuted. Whereas affliction no permitted from its general you say with regard to labor, which permitted from its general

Rather, not say the punishment for affliction, since one can learn it from labor Just as labor, for which from the general are permitted, is punishable by karet, affliction, which has no permitted from its general not all the more so why is it stated? It is available to compare and learn a verbal analogy: Punishment is stated with regard to affliction, and punishment is stated with regard to labor. Just as for labor punished and warned, so too, for affliction punished and warned.
can be refuted, Whereas labor is observed on Shabbatot and Festivals, you say about affliction, which is not observed on Shabbatot and Festivals?
Ravina said: this tanna derived same same it is available, as, if it were not available, it could be refuted, as we refuted it

No, it is certainly available, five verses are written labor One a warning on the day and one for a warning on the night and one for the punishment on the day; and one for the punishment at night; and one to be available to derive affliction from labor, both during the day and night,

The school of Rabbi Yishmael taught “Affliction” is stated here, and “affliction” is stated later, Just as there, did not punish unless there was warning, so too here, did not punish unless there was warning. Rav Aḥa bar Ya’akov said Derive “Shabbat of solemn rest” from Shabbat of Creation. Just as there, did not punish unless there was warning, so too here, did not punish unless there was warning.
Rav Pappa said:
